question: What actions should be taken when two power-driven vessels are meeting head-on?
key_points: Rule 14 applies — head-on situation
Each vessel shall alter course to starboard
Pass port to port
follow_ups: What if the other vessel doesn't alter?
At what angle does a head-on situation become a crossing situation?
examiner_notes: Candidates should reference Rule 14 specifically. Common mistake: forgetting that this rule applies when vessels are in sight of one another.

question: A power-driven vessel is crossing from your starboard side. What are your obligations?
key_points: Rule 15 — crossing situation
You are the give-way vessel when the other is on your starboard side
Avoid crossing ahead of the other vessel
Rule 16 — take early and substantial action
follow_ups: What should the stand-on vessel do if the give-way vessel fails to act?
When does Rule 17(b) permit the stand-on vessel to take action?
examiner_notes: Ensure candidates understand the starboard-side priority rule. They should explain the difference between give-way and stand-on obligations clearly.

question: Describe the lights and shapes displayed by a vessel restricted in her ability to manoeuvre.
key_points: Rule 27(b) applies
Three all-round lights in a vertical line: red-white-red
By day: ball-diamond-ball shape in a vertical line
Masthead, sidelights and sternlight when making way
follow_ups: How do these differ from a vessel not under command?
Give three examples of vessels restricted in their ability to manoeuvre.
examiner_notes: Candidates often confuse RAM with NUC lights. Key distinction: NUC shows two red all-round lights, RAM shows red-white-red. Examples from Rule 3(g) should be cited.

question: What constitutes a narrow channel under the COLREGs and what are the obligations of vessels navigating in one?
key_points: Rule 9 governs narrow channels
Keep as near to the outer limit of the channel on the starboard side as is safe and practicable
Vessels less than 20m or sailing vessels shall not impede vessels that can only navigate within the channel
Overtaking in a narrow channel requires agreement via sound signals
follow_ups: What sound signals are used for overtaking in a narrow channel?
Does Rule 9 override Rule 15 in a crossing situation within a narrow channel?
examiner_notes: Candidates should understand the hierarchy of rules. The 'shall not impede' obligation in Rule 9(b) and (c) is distinct from give-way obligations. Strong candidates will discuss the interplay between Rules 9 and 15.

question: What is a safe speed and what factors must be taken into account when determining it?
key_points: Rule 6 defines safe speed
Must allow proper and effective action to avoid collision
Must allow stopping within an appropriate distance
Factors include visibility, traffic density, manoeuvrability, background lights, wind/sea/current, draught vs available depth
Additional factors when radar is available: characteristics and limitations of radar, sea state effects on detection, effect of vessel's own activities on radar
follow_ups: How does radar availability affect determination of safe speed?
Can a vessel ever be considered to be proceeding at a safe speed while at full ahead?
examiner_notes: Rule 6 lists factors for all vessels and additional factors for vessels with operational radar. Candidates should be able to list at least four factors from each category.

question: Describe the fog signals required by a power-driven vessel making way through the water in restricted visibility.
key_points: Rule 35(a) applies
One prolonged blast at intervals of not more than 2 minutes
A prolonged blast is 4 to 6 seconds duration
Signal is made by whistle
follow_ups: How does the signal change if the vessel is underway but stopped and not making way?
What signal would a vessel not under command make in restricted visibility?
examiner_notes: Candidates frequently confuse 'underway but not making way' (two prolonged blasts) with 'making way' (one prolonged blast). Emphasise the distinction.

question: A vessel is overtaking another in open waters. Describe the responsibilities of each vessel.
key_points: Rule 13 — overtaking situation
Overtaking vessel is the give-way vessel
Coming up from more than 22.5 degrees abaft the beam
The obligation remains until finally past and clear
If in doubt whether overtaking or crossing, assume overtaking
follow_ups: Can the overtaking vessel subsequently become a crossing vessel if the situation changes?
What lights would you see to determine you are overtaking?
examiner_notes: Rule 13(d) is critical — once an overtaking vessel, always an overtaking vessel for that encounter. Candidates should note the 22.5 degree sector and reference seeing only the sternlight.

question: Explain the responsibilities between a sailing vessel and a vessel engaged in fishing when risk of collision exists.
key_points: Rule 18 — responsibilities between vessels
A power-driven vessel shall keep out of the way of a sailing vessel and a vessel engaged in fishing
A sailing vessel shall keep out of the way of a vessel engaged in fishing
The fishing vessel has priority as it is more restricted in manoeuvrability
These rules do not apply in narrow channels, TSS, or restricted visibility
follow_ups: What constitutes 'engaged in fishing' under the COLREGs?
Does a vessel trolling with lines count as engaged in fishing?
examiner_notes: Rule 3(d) defines fishing as using apparatus that restricts manoeuvrability — trolling lines do not qualify. Candidates should know the full hierarchy: NUC > RAM > CBD > fishing > sailing > power-driven.
